title: Food Coloring on Cotton
stainChemistry: Synthetic food dyes (FD&C Red 40, Blue 1, Yellow 5) are water-soluble acid dyes designed for maximum color intensity. On cotton, they bond through ionic and hydrogen bonds. Fresh food coloring is highly water-soluble. However, once dried or heat-set, the dye bonds become increasingly resistant. Some food dyes (Red 40 especially) are notoriously persistent on porous materials.
whyThisWorks: Immediate cold water flushing removes the majority of fresh food dye from cotton because these dyes are designed to be water-soluble. For set stains, oxygen bleach breaks the chromophore bonds. White vinegar helps restore pH after treatment. The speed of initial treatment is the single biggest factor — a 5-minute delay can dramatically increase difficulty.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply amyl acetate or appropriate solvent to dissolve dye/pigment matrix — 5-10 min dwell time
2. Apply NSD to lift loosened pigment. Flush with cool water — 3-5 min dwell time
3. If color persists: apply H2O2 6% on colorfast fabrics to break chromophore bonds — 5-15 min dwell time
4. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse — 1-2 min
1. Blot immediately — never rub (spreading dye worsens it)
2. Apply Dawn dish soap mixed with cold water. Blot gently — 5-10 min dwell time
3. If stain persists: apply hydrogen peroxide 3% on colorfast fabrics — test hidden area first, 5-10 min dwell time
5. Air dry — no heat until stain is completely gone
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 attempts. Take to a professional — they have specialized dye-stripping agents
warnings: Never rub dye stains — rubbing spreads the color

neverDo: No prolonged acid exposure on colored cotton
No chlorine bleach on colored cotton — strips dyes
fiberSensitivities: Test colorfastness on colored cotton before peroxide

escalation.when: If the dye has penetrated the fiber (no improvement after 3 solvent passes), or if the fabric is expensive/delicate, or if dye continues to spread during treatment
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is food coloring on cotton, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Is the color still actively spreading or has it dried?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
